% ----------------------------- Abstract --------------------------------------
\begin{abstract}
\noindent
Soaring birds and human pilots stay aloft by extracting energy from atmospheric updrafts,
producing long, intermittent trajectories that alternate climbs within thermals with
quasi-ballistic glides. Such motion is a candidate for \emph{anomalous transport}, that is,
a departure from ordinary Brownian diffusion. This thesis characterizes the transport
statistics of real soaring flights from a large empirical dataset and compares them with
stochastic transport models.

This document reports the current state of the project. A first, reproducible acquisition
has collected \num{\StatTotalFlights} flights from the FFVL Coupe F\'ed\'erale de Distance
across two disciplines: \num{\StatParaTotalFlights} paraglider flights (seasons
\StatParaSeasonFirst\ to \StatParaSeasonLast) and \num{\StatHangTotalFlights} hang-glider
flights (\StatHangSeasonFirst\ to \StatHangSeasonLast). Of these, \num{\StatTotalWithIgc}
carry a GPS tracklog and \num{\StatTotalDownloaded} have been downloaded and verified. Data
collection is ongoing and may be extended to further public soaring databases -- notably
WeGlide for sailplanes, and XContest -- using the same approach. The remainder of the
document describes the acquisition method, the dataset and its statistics, and the planned
analysis and modelling.
\end{abstract}
